1:
On Colossians 2:1 — “For I would that ye knew what great conflict I have for you, and for them at Laodicea, and for as many as have not seen my face in the flesh” — the witnesses mark Paul’s strenuous care for churches he had not seen.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word. Robertson: “how great a contest I am having” (helikon agona), an inward struggle of anxiety. Ellicott: the same word as the “striving” before (Philippians 1:30). The Puritan Henry: he cared for Colosse though he had “never been” there. The Reformed Calvin: he declares his affection to gain credit and authority.

The verse opens Paul’s appeal with a confession of deep concern. He has great conflict — an inward agony of care and prayer — for the Colossians, the Laodiceans, and all who had never met him. His love reaches even to strangers.

So the witnesses see Paul lay bare the depth of his pastoral concern. The witnesses note the strong word “conflict” — an inward contest, a wrestling of anxiety and prayer, the same striving he had spoken of at the close of the previous chapter. They mark the breadth of his care: not only for Colosse, but for nearby Laodicea, and indeed for “as many as have not seen my face” — churches he had never personally visited, yet for whom he labored as tenderly as for his own converts. They observe Calvin’s point that Paul opens with this declaration of affection to win their trust, for we readily believe those we know to be concerned for our good. They note that his concern was no idle sentiment but an agonizing struggle, carried on even amid his own dangers and imprisonment. The verse thus sets the affectionate tone of the warning to come: Paul’s great striving is that these believers, exposed to false teaching, might be established in the truth. His love for unseen churches, wrestling for their steadfastness, is the spring of the doctrinal defense and the warnings against error that fill the chapter.

So the witnesses see Paul lay bare the depth of his pastoral concern. The strong word “conflict” marks an inward contest, a wrestling of anxiety and prayer. They mark the breadth of his care: not only Colosse, but Laodicea, and all who had never seen his face — churches he had never visited, yet loved as his own. Calvin’s point: he opens with this declaration of affection to win their trust, for we readily believe those concerned for our good. His striving is no idle sentiment but an agonizing struggle, the spring of the warnings to come.
2:
On Colossians 2:2 — “That their hearts might be comforted, being knit together in love, and unto all riches of the full assurance of understanding, to the acknowledgement of the mystery of God… and of Christ” — the witnesses mark the aim of his striving.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Robertson: “comforted” (paraklethosin); “knit together” (synbibasthentes, to make go together, coalesce; cf. Ephesians 4:16). Wesley: “unto the fullest and clearest… knowledge of the gospel.” The Reformed Calvin: he desires nothing but that they be “united together in faith and love.”

The verse states the goal of Paul’s conflict. He strives that their hearts be encouraged, knit together in love, and brought to the full riches of assured understanding, knowing the mystery of God — which is Christ. Love and assured knowledge are his aim.

So the witnesses see Paul name the threefold object of his striving for these churches. The witnesses note the first: “that their hearts might be comforted” — encouraged and strengthened, that they stand fast and advance; the second: “being knit together in love” — welded and compacted into one body by mutual love, as beams are joined in a building. They mark the third and highest: “unto all riches of the full assurance of understanding” — that they reach the abundant, settled certainty of grasping the gospel, no longer wavering but fully assured. They observe that the climax of this knowledge is “the mystery of God… even Christ” — Christ himself is the mystery once hidden, now revealed, the sum and centre of all true knowledge. They note that Paul thus sets a high spiritual goal against the false teachers’ pretended wisdom: not their speculations, but love and the full assurance of Christ. The verse establishes what Paul longs for the Colossians to have — hearts encouraged, united in love, and rich in the certain knowledge of Christ the mystery of God — a fullness that leaves no room for the empty additions of error, and that rests, as the next verse declares, on the treasures hidden in Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul name the threefold object of his striving. First, hearts “comforted” — encouraged and strengthened; second, “knit together in love” — welded into one body, as beams joined in a building; third and highest, “all riches of the full assurance of understanding” — the abundant, settled certainty of grasping the gospel. The climax is “the mystery of God… even Christ” — Christ himself the once-hidden mystery, the sum of all true knowledge. Against the false teachers’ speculations, Paul longs for hearts united in love and rich in the certain knowledge of Christ.
3:
On Colossians 2:3 — “In whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” — the witnesses mark the treasures hidden in Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” — in Christ, who is the mystery of God. Ellicott: “hidden treasures” — apocryphi, an almost technical word for secret teaching, here turned against the false “wisdom.” Adam Clarke: in Christianity “all the treasures… of wisdom and knowledge are contained.”

The verse locates all true wisdom in Christ. In him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge. The believer needs seek wisdom nowhere else.

So the witnesses see Paul concentrate all wisdom and knowledge in Christ alone. The witnesses note the comprehensiveness: “all the treasures” — the whole abundance and excellency of wisdom and knowledge — are laid up in Christ as in a storehouse. They mark the word “hid”: not that the treasures are concealed from believers, but that they are stored up and deposited in Christ, to be found in him and nowhere else; what is hidden from the proud and the false teachers is opened to those who seek it in Christ. They observe Ellicott’s point that the very word for “hidden” was the term the false teachers used for their secret, esoteric lore — and Paul turns it against them: the only true hidden treasure is in Christ, not in their speculations. They note that this is the foundation of the warning: since all wisdom is in Christ, to seek it elsewhere — in philosophy, tradition, or angelic mediators — is folly and loss. The verse thus arms the Colossians against the seduction of a higher “wisdom”: Christ is the sole repository of all true wisdom and knowledge, and the believer who has him has all. The deceivers’ enticing words offer nothing that is not already richer and fuller in Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul concentrate all wisdom and knowledge in Christ alone. “All the treasures” — the whole abundance of wisdom and knowledge — are laid up in him as in a storehouse. The word “hid” means stored and deposited in Christ, to be found in him and nowhere else; what is hidden from the proud is opened to those who seek it in him. Ellicott’s point: the very word for “hidden” was the false teachers’ term for their secret lore, turned against them. Since all wisdom is in Christ, to seek it elsewhere is folly; the believer who has him has all.
4:
On Colossians 2:4 — “And this I say, lest any man should beguile you with enticing words” — the witnesses mark the purpose of the foregoing.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the danger. Robertson: “may delude” (paralogizetai), to reason aside, cheat by false reasoning. Adam Clarke: to “deceive by sophistry… the premises… assumed without evidence.” Ellicott: “enticing words” are words of persuasion rather than reason. The Puritan Henry: he insists on Christ’s perfection to preserve them from “ensnaring insinuations.”

The verse gives the reason for exalting Christ. Paul says all this lest anyone deceive them with persuasive words. The fullness of Christ is the safeguard against plausible error.

So the witnesses see Paul reveal the protective purpose behind his exaltation of Christ. The witnesses note the word for the danger: to “beguile” is to delude by false reasoning, to cheat by sophistry — arguments that seem to follow soundly but rest on premises assumed or false. They mark the instrument: “enticing words” — persuasive, plausible speech, the smooth rhetoric of the false teachers, designed to charm rather than to prove. They observe that Paul has pressed the all-sufficiency of Christ (vv. 2–3) precisely as an antidote: the mind preoccupied with the persuasion that the knowledge of Christ is amply sufficient cannot be drawn aside by clever speech offering some supposed addition. They note Calvin’s point that this conviction is the key that closes the door against all errors. The verse thus states the aim of the whole passage: not abstract theology but pastoral defense — that the Colossians, secure in the fullness of Christ, might not be talked out of it by the seductive eloquence of error. It introduces directly the warnings against false philosophy and Judaizing observance that follow, all of which are answered by the sufficiency of Christ already affirmed.

So the witnesses see Paul reveal the protective purpose behind his exaltation of Christ. To “beguile” is to delude by false reasoning, to cheat by sophistry — arguments that seem sound but rest on false premises. The instrument is “enticing words” — persuasive, plausible speech designed to charm. Paul has pressed the all-sufficiency of Christ precisely as antidote: the mind convinced that the knowledge of Christ is amply sufficient cannot be drawn aside by clever speech. Calvin’s point: this conviction is the key that closes the door against all errors — pastoral defense, not abstract theology.
5:
On Colossians 2:5 — “For though I be absent in the flesh, yet am I with you in the spirit, joying and beholding your order, and the stedfastness of your faith in Christ” — the witnesses mark Paul’s spiritual presence and joy.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the military terms. Robertson: “your order” (taxin), the military line, unbroken; “stedfastness” (stereoma), solidity, like a solid front. Ellicott: “order… and steadfastness, or solidity.” The Reformed Calvin: by praising their present condition he admonishes them not to fall away.

The verse expresses Paul’s presence in spirit and his joy at their state. Though absent in body, he is with them in spirit, rejoicing to see their good order and the firmness of their faith. He commends what he would have them keep.

So the witnesses see Paul, though far away, rejoice over the Colossians’ steadfastness. The witnesses note the paradox: “absent in the flesh,” yet “with you in the spirit” — his affection and concern made him as one present, beholding their condition as if before his eyes. They mark the military imagery the expositors draw out: “your order” — like soldiers in unbroken rank, their fellowship disciplined and intact; and “the stedfastness of your faith” — a solid, firm front, like a fortified line that holds. They observe that, though a few stragglers may have drifted toward the false teaching, there had been no general defection; the church still stood firm. They note Calvin’s point that Paul commends their present steadfastness precisely to encourage them to maintain it — praise as a spur to perseverance. The verse thus blends affection, joy, and gentle exhortation: Paul rejoices to see their ordered, steadfast faith, and by rejoicing in it, urges them to keep it. His commendation prepares the great exhortation that follows — to walk on in the Christ they had received, rooted and built up in him, against every effort to spoil them.

So the witnesses see Paul, though far away, rejoice over the Colossians’ steadfastness. The paradox: “absent in the flesh,” yet “with you in the spirit” — his concern making him as one present. The military imagery: “your order” — like soldiers in unbroken rank; and “the stedfastness of your faith” — a solid front that holds. Though a few stragglers may have drifted, there had been no general defection. Calvin’s point: Paul commends their steadfastness to encourage them to maintain it — praise as a spur to perseverance, preparing the great exhortation to walk on in Christ.
6:
On Colossians 2:6 — “As ye have therefore received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk ye in him” — the witnesses mark the call to continue in the Christ received.

The Eastern fathers note the appeal to their own profession. Chrysostom: “we introduce no strange addition… Walk ye in Him, for He is the Way.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “ye received” (parelabete) Christ as the tradition, “Christ Jesus the Lord” the full title. The Reformed Calvin: their having received Christ profits nothing unless they remain in him.

The verse states the governing exhortation. As you received Christ Jesus the Lord, so go on walking in him. The remedy against error is steadfast continuance in the Christ already received.

So the witnesses see Paul lay down the great rule that answers all the false teaching: continue in the Christ you received. The witnesses note the appeal to their own beginning — “as ye have received Christ Jesus the Lord” — Christ in his fullness, as Saviour and Sovereign Lord, was the message they had embraced; they are simply to keep on as they began. They mark Chrysostom’s point that Paul brings no “strange addition” — unlike the false teachers, who add to Christ; the apostle calls them only to walk in the Christ they already have, for he is the Way. They observe the full title, “Christ Jesus the Lord” — the very confession that excludes every rival mediator and every supplement. They note Calvin’s warning that to have received Christ once is of no advantage unless one remains in him; perseverance, not mere beginning, is what saves. The verse thus sets the theme of the rest of the chapter and the next: the answer to the seductions of philosophy, ritual, and angel-worship is not some new attainment but faithful continuance in Christ as first received. To walk in him — rooted, built up, established — is the whole of the Christian’s safety and growth.

So the witnesses see Paul lay down the great rule that answers all the false teaching: continue in the Christ you received. The appeal is to their own beginning — Christ in his fullness, as Saviour and Sovereign Lord, was the message they embraced; they are to keep on as they began. Chrysostom’s point: Paul brings no “strange addition” unlike the false teachers, who add to Christ. The full title “Christ Jesus the Lord” excludes every rival mediator. Calvin warns that to have received Christ once profits nothing unless one remains in him — perseverance, not mere beginning, saves.
7:
On Colossians 2:7 — “Rooted and built up in him, and stablished in the faith, as ye have been taught, abounding therein with thanksgiving” — the witnesses mark the metaphors of growth and stability.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the mixed figures. Robertson: “rooted” (errizomenoi, perfect, rooted to stay), changing from walk to tree; “builded up” (epoikodomoumenoi, present, ongoing). Ellicott: “having been rooted” once, “being built up continually” (Ephesians 3:17). Adam Clarke notes the double metaphor of tree and building.

The verse describes the walk in Christ by vivid figures. They are to be rooted in him like a tree, built up like a house, established in the faith, abounding in thanksgiving. Depth, growth, firmness, and gratitude mark the life in Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul unfold the “walk in him” through a cluster of metaphors. The witnesses note the shift from the figure of walking to that of a tree: “rooted” in Christ — the perfect tense marking a settled state, rooted once for all and staying so, drawing all nourishment from him as the soil. They mark the second figure, from architecture: “built up in him” — a present, ongoing process, rising continually as a building upon its foundation. They observe the plain restatement: “stablished in the faith, as ye have been taught” — made firm and steadfast in the doctrine they had received, not carried off to novelties. They note the crowning grace: “abounding therein with thanksgiving” — overflowing with gratitude, the mark of a heart satisfied in Christ. They observe how the tenses combine the settled and the growing: rooted (done) yet built up (continuing), established yet abounding. The verse thus paints the healthy life in Christ that is the antidote to error: deeply rooted, ever growing, firmly established in the received faith, and overflowing with thankfulness — a fullness in Christ that needs nothing the false teachers offer, and is now set sharply against their empty philosophy.

So the witnesses see Paul unfold the “walk in him” through a cluster of metaphors. “Rooted” in Christ — the perfect tense marking a settled state, rooted once for all, drawing nourishment from him as the soil. “Built up” — a present, ongoing process, rising continually on the foundation. “Stablished in the faith, as ye have been taught” — made firm in the received doctrine. And the crowning grace, “abounding therein with thanksgiving” — overflowing with gratitude. The tenses combine the settled and the growing: rooted yet built up, established yet abounding — a fullness in Christ that needs nothing the false teachers offer.
8:
On Colossians 2:8 — “Beware lest any man spoil you through philosophy and vain deceit, after the tradition of men, after the rudiments of the world, and not after Christ” — the witnesses mark the warning against false philosophy.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the plunder. Robertson: “take heed” (blepete), beware; “maketh spoil” (sylagogon), lead away as booty. Ellicott: “lead you away as a spoil… make you a slave.” Wesley: not philosophy itself but “the empty deceit of philosophy blended with Christianity.” The Puritan Henry distinguishes true philosophy from the “vain and deceitful.”

The verse warns against being carried off by false teaching. Beware lest anyone plunder you through philosophy and empty deceit, built on human tradition and worldly principles, not on Christ. The test is whether teaching is “after Christ.”

So the witnesses see Paul issue the central warning of the chapter against the false teachers. The witnesses note the vivid verb: to “spoil” is to carry off as plunder, to kidnap and enslave — the deceivers would rob the Colossians of the treasure of Christ and lead them captive. They mark the careful target: not philosophy as such — for, as Henry notes, there is a true study of God’s works that confirms faith — but “philosophy and vain deceit,” the empty, deceptive speculation that, blended with Christianity, corrupts it. They observe its threefold character: “after the tradition of men” — resting on human invention, not divine revelation; “after the rudiments of the world” — the elementary, worldly principles, whether pagan or ceremonial; and the decisive mark, “not after Christ.” They note that this last is the test of all teaching: whatever is not according to Christ, however learned or plausible, is to be rejected. The verse thus sounds the alarm: the danger is real and the loss grave, but the criterion is simple — any teaching that draws the soul from Christ, resting on human tradition and worldly elements rather than on him, is spoil and deceit. Against it Paul will set the fullness of the Godhead in Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul issue the central warning of the chapter. To “spoil” is to carry off as plunder, to kidnap and enslave — the deceivers would rob the Colossians of the treasure of Christ. They mark the careful target: not philosophy as such (true study confirms faith, Henry), but “philosophy and vain deceit,” the empty speculation that, blended with Christianity, corrupts it. Its threefold character: “after the tradition of men,” “after the rudiments of the world,” and the decisive mark, “not after Christ.” This last is the test of all teaching: whatever draws the soul from Christ is spoil and deceit.
9:
On Colossians 2:9 — “For in him dwelleth all the fulness of the Godhead bodily” — the witnesses mark the great Christological affirmation.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh every word. Robertson: this states “the heart of his message about the person of Christ” — “the fulness of the Godhead” (theotetos, deity itself) dwells (katoikei, abides permanently) in him “bodily” (somatikos). Ellicott: “all the fulness… not a mere emanation… dwells and remains forever… bodily.” Wesley: “not only divine powers, but divine nature.”

The verse gives the ground of all: the fullness of Deity dwells in Christ bodily. The whole of the Godhead resides permanently and personally in him. Against every error, this is the heart of the gospel.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the all-sufficiency of Christ in his full deity. The witnesses note the weight of every word against the false teachers, who made Christ one among many emanations: “all the fulness” — the entire completeness, nothing lacking; “of the Godhead” — not mere divine influence or power but the very Deity, the divine nature itself; “dwelleth” — abides permanently and continually, not descending for a time and departing; and “bodily” — in his incarnate person, in bodily form, the whole Godhead united to the manhood. They mark that this is the centre of Paul’s answer to the heresy: since the entire fullness of God resides in Christ, there is no need and no room for supplements, mediators, or emanations; he is no partial revelation but God himself in the flesh. They observe Wesley’s precision: not only divine powers but the divine nature dwells in him. They note the contrast with the believer, who is “filled” from Christ, while in Christ the fullness dwells originally and essentially. The verse thus states the supreme truth that overturns all false teaching: in Christ dwells all the fullness of the Godhead bodily — and therefore, as the next verse declares, the believer is complete in him.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the all-sufficiency of Christ in his full deity. Every word weighs against the false teachers, who made Christ one among many emanations: “all the fulness” — entire completeness; “of the Godhead” — the very Deity, the divine nature itself, not mere influence; “dwelleth” — abides permanently; and “bodily” — in his incarnate person. Since the entire fullness of God resides in Christ, there is no room for supplements, mediators, or emanations; he is God himself in the flesh. This is the centre of Paul’s answer to the heresy, and the ground of the believer’s completeness.
10:
On Colossians 2:10 — “And ye are complete in him, which is the head of all principality and power” — the witnesses mark the believer’s completeness in Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word. Robertson: “ye are made full” (pepleromenoi) — “Christ is our fulness of which we all partake” (John 1:16). Adam Clarke: “ye are filled with him” — the philosophy was empty, Christ is full. Ellicott: “filled up in His fulness” (Ephesians 3:19). The Reformed Calvin: in him we possess “an entire perfection.”

The verse draws the conclusion for the believer. You are complete in Christ, the head of all rule and authority. Having him who is the fullness of God, the believer lacks nothing.

So the witnesses see Paul apply the fullness of Christ to the believer’s state. The witnesses note the inference: since all the fullness of the Godhead dwells in Christ, those who are in him are “complete” — filled full, made complete, lacking nothing for salvation or perfection. They mark Clarke’s sharp contrast: the false philosophy was empty (kene), but Christ is the fullness (pleroma); the Colossians, once empty, are filled in him. They observe that the believer’s completeness is derived — we are filled from Christ’s fullness, as he is filled with God — yet real and entire: in him we possess an entire perfection, needing no addition from human tradition or angelic mediation. They note the crowning title: Christ is “the head of all principality and power” — supreme over every angelic rank and authority, the very beings the false teachers would have them worship. Since Christ is head over all angels, to seek them as mediators is to dishonor the Head and forsake the fullness. The verse thus completes the argument: the believer, united to the all-full Christ who is Lord of every power, is himself complete — and therefore needs nothing the deceivers offer. This completeness is unfolded in the spiritual circumcision and resurrection that follow.

So the witnesses see Paul apply the fullness of Christ to the believer’s state. Since all the fullness of the Godhead dwells in Christ, those in him are “complete” — filled full, lacking nothing for salvation or perfection. Clarke’s contrast: the philosophy was empty (kene), but Christ is the fullness (pleroma); the once-empty are filled in him. The crowning title: Christ is “the head of all principality and power” — supreme over every angelic rank, the very beings the false teachers would worship. Since Christ is head over all angels, to seek them as mediators is to dishonor the Head and forsake the fullness.
11:
On Colossians 2:11 — “In whom also ye are circumcised with the circumcision made without hands, in putting off the body of the sins of the flesh by the circumcision of Christ” — the witnesses mark the spiritual circumcision.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the metaphor. Robertson: “circumcised” used spiritually, “circumcision of the heart” (Romans 2:29); “not made with hands” (acheiropoieto). Adam Clarke: not cutting off flesh but “putting off the body of the sins of the flesh.” The Reformed Calvin: against the false apostles who mixed law with gospel — Mosaic circumcision is unnecessary.

The verse answers the demand for circumcision. In Christ believers have a circumcision not made with hands — the putting off of the sinful nature, Christ’s own spiritual circumcision. The outward rite is fulfilled in an inward reality.

So the witnesses see Paul meet the Judaizing demand for circumcision with the inward reality it foreshadowed. The witnesses note that the false teachers, like the Jews, made circumcision necessary to perfection; Paul replies that believers already possess the true circumcision in Christ. They mark its nature: “made without hands” — not the cutting of flesh by human instrument, but a spiritual operation; “putting off the body of the sins of the flesh” — the stripping away, not of a little skin, but of the whole sinful nature, the body of corruption. They observe that this is “the circumcision of Christ” — the circumcision he performs, or which belongs to him, wrought by his Spirit and power, the inward renewal that the outward rite only signified. They note Calvin’s point that this proves Mosaic circumcision needless under Christ, for the substance has come. They observe that to be complete in Christ (v. 10) includes having the true circumcision; the believer needs no fleshly rite, having the spiritual reality. The verse thus disarms the Judaizers: the Colossians, uncircumcised in flesh, are circumcised in heart by Christ — the putting off of sin that the law’s sign pointed to, now fulfilled inwardly. This spiritual circumcision is then linked to baptism and resurrection in the verses following.
12:
On Colossians 2:12 — “Buried with him in baptism, wherein also ye are risen with him through the faith of the operation of God, who hath raised him from the dead” — the witnesses mark burial and resurrection with Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “buried with him” (syntaphentes, cf. Romans 6:4); the plunging under and emerging. Adam Clarke and Wesley note the allusion to immersion. Ellicott compares Romans 6:4 — risen “to walk in newness of life.” The Reformed Calvin: by baptism we are partakers of Christ’s death and resurrection.

The verse links the spiritual circumcision to baptism. In baptism believers were buried with Christ and raised with him through faith in God’s power. The rite portrays death to sin and new life.

So the witnesses see Paul connect the inward circumcision with baptism, the sign of union with Christ. The witnesses note the double figure: “buried with him in baptism” — the believer, united to Christ, shares his burial, the old life laid in the grave; and “risen with him” — raised to new life, as Christ rose. They mark the allusion the expositors draw to the ancient mode of immersion, the plunging under the water picturing burial and the emerging picturing resurrection — though, as Wesley notes, no stress is laid on the mode for the doctrine’s sake. They observe the means: “through the faith of the operation of God” — it is not the water but faith in the mighty working of God, who raised Christ, that effects the rising; baptism signifies, faith receives. They note Calvin’s point that baptism makes us partakers of Christ’s death and resurrection, displacing circumcision as the sign of the covenant. They observe that this completes the answer to the Judaizers: the Colossians have in baptism, by faith, the death to sin and new life that the law’s rites could not give. The verse thus roots the believer’s new life in union with the dead and risen Christ — a union by faith, signified in baptism, that the next verse traces to God’s quickening of the dead.
13:
On Colossians 2:13 — “And you, being dead in your sins and the uncircumcision of your flesh, hath he quickened together with him, having forgiven you all trespasses” — the witnesses mark the quickening of the dead.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the application. Robertson: “and you” emphatic, Gentiles; “being dead through your trespasses” (moral death, Ephesians 2:1,5). The Puritan Henry: “Christ’s death is our life.” Wesley: “doubly dead… in the uncircumcision of your flesh,” original sin. The Reformed Calvin applies the general truth to themselves.

The verse brings the truth home to the Gentile believers. Though dead in sins and uncircumcised, God made them alive with Christ, forgiving all their trespasses. Spiritual death gives way to life and pardon.

So the witnesses see Paul apply the death-and-resurrection to the Colossians’ own case, the most effectual teaching. The witnesses note the prior state: “dead in your sins” — morally dead, separated from God, as a corpse is from life; “and the uncircumcision of your flesh” — both their Gentile state, outside the covenant, and, as Wesley reads it, the uncircumcised, corrupt nature within; they were doubly dead. They mark the divine act: “hath he quickened together with him” — God made them alive with the same life by which he raised Christ, a sharing in his resurrection. They observe the ground and means: “having forgiven you all trespasses” — the quickening is joined to a full and free pardon of every sin, for there is no life without forgiveness. They note that this is the heart of the gospel applied: those who were dead and condemned are made alive and forgiven, wholly by God’s grace in Christ. They observe how this answers the false teaching: the Colossians owe their life and pardon not to ritual or philosophy but to God’s quickening grace in the crucified and risen Christ. The verse thus sets the stage for the great declaration of how that forgiveness was achieved — the blotting out of the law’s condemning bond, nailed to the cross.

So the witnesses see Paul apply the death-and-resurrection to the Colossians’ own case. Their prior state: “dead in your sins” — morally dead, separated from God; “and the uncircumcision of your flesh” — both their Gentile state and, as Wesley reads it, the corrupt nature within; doubly dead. The divine act: “hath he quickened together with him” — made alive with the life by which God raised Christ. The ground and means: “having forgiven you all trespasses” — the quickening joined to a full, free pardon, for there is no life without forgiveness. Those dead and condemned are made alive and forgiven, wholly by grace.
14:
On Colossians 2:14 — “Blotting out the handwriting of ordinances that was against us, which was contrary to us, and took it out of the way, nailing it to his cross” — the witnesses mark the cancellation of the law’s bond.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “having blotted out” (exaleipsas), to wipe off, erase, cancel. Ellicott: the “handwriting” is the bond exacting payment (Philemon 1:19). Adam Clarke: “the ceremonial law… against them… contrary to them.” The Reformed Calvin: the ceremonies a bond by which God held men guilty.

The verse declares how forgiveness was wrought. God cancelled the written bond of ordinances that condemned us, removed it, and nailed it to the cross. The law’s accusing debt is abolished in Christ’s death.

So the witnesses see Paul portray the abolition of the law’s condemning power at the cross. The witnesses note the central figure: a “handwriting” — a bond, a signed certificate of debt, like a note acknowledging an obligation. They mark its character: “of ordinances” — the law, especially its ceremonial requirements, which testified against the sinner; “against us… contrary to us” — standing as a hostile witness, exacting the penalty we could not pay. They observe the threefold act of God in Christ: he “blotted it out” — wiped the writing clean, cancelling the debt; “took it out of the way” — removed the obstacle entirely; and “nailing it to his cross” — a vivid image, the bond fastened to the cross with Christ, cancelled in his crucified body. They note Calvin’s point that the ceremonies were the very bond by which God held men guilty, now abolished. They observe how this crowns the answer to the Judaizers: the law’s requirements that condemned the sinner are not merely set aside but destroyed in Christ’s death; there remains no bond to enforce them. The verse thus declares the ground of the full forgiveness of verse 13: the accusing law, with its condemning ordinances, is wiped out and nailed to the cross — and the triumph over the powers that wielded it follows.

So the witnesses see Paul portray the abolition of the law’s condemning power at the cross. The central figure: a “handwriting” — a bond, a signed certificate of debt. Its character: “of ordinances” — the law, especially ceremonial, that testified against the sinner; “against us… contrary to us” — a hostile witness exacting the penalty. The threefold act of God in Christ: he “blotted it out” (wiped the writing clean), “took it out of the way” (removed the obstacle), and “nailing it to his cross” (cancelled in Christ’s crucified body). The accusing law is not set aside but destroyed in Christ’s death.
15:
On Colossians 2:15 — “And having spoiled principalities and powers, he made a shew of them openly, triumphing over them in it” — the witnesses mark the triumph over the powers.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the military imagery and a crux. Robertson: “having put off from himself” (apekdysamenos) — two problems: is it God or Christ, and the meaning of the verb; “made a show” like a triumph. Adam Clarke: the conquered “spoiled of their armour… exhibited… to the populace.” The Reformed Calvin: the devils are disarmed, so they cannot accuse us.

The verse declares Christ’s victory over evil powers. Having disarmed the principalities and powers, he displayed them openly, triumphing over them by the cross. The very death that paid our debt routed our enemies.

So the witnesses see Paul crown the work of the cross with the triumph over the powers of darkness. The witnesses note the military imagery of a Roman triumph: Christ “spoiled” the principalities and powers — the hostile spiritual rulers, the devils — stripping them of their arms, as a conqueror despoils the vanquished. They mark Calvin’s point that this disarming means they can no longer accuse us, for the bond of our guilt that they wielded is destroyed (v. 14). They observe the public spectacle: he “made a shew of them openly” — exhibited them as captives in a victory procession; “triumphing over them in it” — in the cross (or in himself), the very instrument of apparent defeat made the scene of his decisive victory. They note the interpretive cruxes the expositors raise: whether the subject is God or Christ (it comes to the same, the Father acting in the Son), and whether the verb means “put off from himself” the powers or “stripped” them — either way, the powers are defeated and despoiled. They observe the paradox that the cross, which looked like Christ’s humiliation, was his triumph over every evil power. The verse thus completes the argument against the false teachers: since Christ has conquered and disarmed the very angelic powers, to worship or fear them is folly — the believer is on the side of the Victor.
16:
On Colossians 2:16 — “Let no man therefore judge you in meat, or in drink, or in respect of an holyday, or of the new moon, or of the sabbath days” — the witnesses mark the freedom from ritual judgment.

The Eastern fathers note the application. Chrysostom: having warned in general, he now applies it. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “let no one judge you” (krineto) — the ascetic rules of one wing of the Gnostics. Adam Clarke: distinctions of clean/unclean meats and drinks, and observance of days. The Puritan Henry: against “judaizing teachers” imposing the ceremonial law.

The verse draws the practical conclusion. Therefore let no one condemn you over food, drink, or the observance of festivals, new moons, or sabbaths. Since the bond is cancelled, the believer is free from these ceremonial judgments.

So the witnesses see Paul apply the cancellation of the law’s bond to the matter of ceremonial observance. The witnesses note the inference: “therefore” — because believers are complete in Christ and the handwriting of ordinances is blotted out, no one is to sit in judgment on them for not keeping the ceremonial requirements. They mark the particulars: “meat… drink” — the distinctions of clean and unclean foods; and the sacred times — the yearly festivals (“holyday”), the monthly new moons, and the weekly sabbaths. They observe that these were the ceremonial observances of the law, now fulfilled and abolished in Christ; to be condemned for neglecting them is to be brought back under a yoke from which Christ has freed. They note that the Colossian error mixed Judaizing ritual with its philosophy, demanding these observances; Paul forbids the church to submit to such judgment. They observe that the freedom is grounded in the cross: the very ordinances are cancelled, so their enforcement has no authority. The verse thus secures the believer’s liberty: complete in Christ, freed by his cross, the Christian is not to be judged or enslaved by the ceremonial law of food and sacred days — for these, as the next verse explains, were only the shadow of which Christ is the substance.
17:
On Colossians 2:17 — “Which are a shadow of things to come; but the body is of Christ” — the witnesses mark the shadow and the substance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast. Robertson: “a shadow” (skia) opposed to substance (soma, body); the body casts the shadow and belongs to Christ. Ellicott compares Hebrews 10:1 — “the Law had a shadow… not the very image.” Adam Clarke: these were types, in force till Christ came. The Reformed Calvin: shadows contrasted with revelation.

The verse explains why the ceremonies bind no longer. They were a shadow of things to come; the substance belongs to Christ. The reality having come, the shadow passes.

So the witnesses see Paul reduce the ceremonial law to a passing shadow whose reality is Christ. The witnesses note the apt figure: the ordinances were “a shadow of things to come” — a dim outline cast ahead by the coming reality, useful in their season as the shadow tells of the approaching object. They mark the contrast: “but the body is of Christ” — the substance, the solid reality that casts the shadow, is found in Christ; the body belongs to him. They observe the logic, the same as in Hebrews: a shadow is valued while the substance is absent, but once the body itself is present, to cling to the shadow is absurd. The ceremonies pointed forward to Christ; now that he has come, their purpose is fulfilled and they pass away. They note Calvin’s point that those who still adhere to the shadows act like one who judges of a man by his shadow when the man himself stands before him. They observe that this secures the freedom of verse 16: the Colossians are not to be judged by ceremonies because these were only the shadow of Christ, who has now come in substance. The verse thus gives the principle that disarms all ritual legalism: the law’s observances were shadows; Christ is the reality, and the believer who has the substance need not return to the shadow.

So the witnesses see Paul reduce the ceremonial law to a passing shadow whose reality is Christ. The ordinances were “a shadow of things to come” — a dim outline cast ahead by the coming reality, useful in their season. The contrast: “but the body is of Christ” — the substance, the solid reality casting the shadow, found in him. The logic, as in Hebrews: a shadow is valued while the substance is absent, but once the body is present, to cling to the shadow is absurd. Calvin’s image: those who still adhere to the shadows judge a man by his shadow while he stands before them. The believer who has the substance needs not the shadow.
18:
On Colossians 2:18 — “Let no man beguile you of your reward in a voluntary humility and worshipping of angels, intruding into those things which he hath not seen, vainly puffed up by his fleshly mind” — the witnesses mark the warning against angel-worship and false humility.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the athletic figure. Robertson: “rob you of your prize” (katabrabeueto), act as umpire against you. Ellicott: the prize is the heavenly reward (Philippians 3:14). The Puritan Henry: it “looked like humility to apply to angels,” but was really pride. Wesley: “this really sprung from their being puffed up.”

The verse warns against a subtle error robbing them of their reward. Let no one disqualify you through affected humility and angel-worship, dwelling on visions, puffed up by a carnal mind. False lowliness masks real pride.

So the witnesses see Paul expose the angel-worship and false humility of the deceivers. The witnesses note the athletic image: to “beguile you of your reward” is to act as an unjust umpire, robbing the runner of his prize — the heavenly reward of the Christian course. They mark the error’s twofold mask: “voluntary humility” — an affected, self-imposed lowliness that pretended men were unworthy to approach God directly; and “worshipping of angels” — applying instead to angelic mediators, as if too humble to come to God through Christ alone. They observe Henry’s and Wesley’s piercing point: this seeming humility was in truth pride, for it robbed Christ of his honor as sole Mediator and rested on the worshippers’ own conceit. They note the further marks: “intruding into those things which he hath not seen” — prying presumptuously into invisible mysteries and speculations; and “vainly puffed up by his fleshly mind” — inflated, despite the show of humility, by a carnal, unspiritual understanding. They observe that the whole system, for all its pious appearance, dethroned Christ and exalted the deceiver. The verse thus unmasks the danger: an affected humility and angel-cult that, while parading lowliness, is proud, presumptuous, and fatal to the believer’s prize — because, as the next verse charges, it lets go of the Head.
19:
On Colossians 2:19 — “And not holding the Head, from which all the body by joints and bands having nourishment ministered, and knit together, increaseth with the increase of God” — the witnesses mark the fatal error of letting go the Head.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the diagnosis. Robertson: “not holding fast the Head” (ou kraton) — actually deserting Christ, the Gnostics dethroning him. Ellicott: “in this lay the fatal error… interfered with the direct hold” on Christ. The Reformed Calvin: whatever does not bear relation to Christ is condemned, for all flows from him. Wesley: “he does not hold Christ, who does not trust in him alone.”

The verse names the root of the error: failing to hold the Head. The false teacher does not hold fast to Christ, from whom the whole body draws life and growth. To let go of Christ is to lose all.

So the witnesses see Paul lay the finger on the fatal flaw of the whole system: it does not hold the Head. The witnesses note that, for all its show of humility and wisdom, the angel-worshipping philosophy “holds not the Head” — lets go of Christ, dethrones him from his sole primacy, setting angels and mediators in his place. They mark Wesley’s point that one does not hold Christ who does not trust in him alone; to add other mediators is to release one’s grip on the Head. They observe the vital figure of the body: from Christ the Head “all the body, by joints and bands” — the ligaments and connections that unite the members — receives “nourishment” and is “knit together,” so that it grows “with the increase of God,” the growth God gives. They note Calvin’s point that all life and growth flow from Christ alone, so that whatever does not relate to him is worthless. They observe the contrast: the false teachers promise growth and perfection apart from Christ, but all true increase comes only from the Head; severed from him, the body withers. The verse thus exposes the error’s ruin: by letting go of Christ, it cuts itself off from the only source of life and growth — and prepares the conclusion that, being dead with Christ, the believer is free from the world’s ordinances.
20:
On Colossians 2:20 — “Wherefore if ye be dead with Christ from the rudiments of the world, why, as though living in the world, are ye subject to ordinances?” — the witnesses mark the argument from death with Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the condition. Robertson: “if ye died” (ei apethanete), assumed true, the burial in baptism (2:12); “the rudiments of the world” (2:8). Adam Clarke: the rites were “rudiments, first elements” renounced. The Reformed Calvin: we are dead to the law and the law to us (Galatians 2:19).

The verse argues from the believer’s death with Christ. Since you died with Christ to the world’s elementary principles, why submit to its ordinances as if still living in it? Death to the world ends its claims.

So the witnesses see Paul press the believer’s death with Christ as an argument against ritual bondage. The witnesses note the assumed fact: “ye are dead with Christ” — not in doubt but taken as true; in union with the crucified Christ, the believer died, as pictured in baptism, to “the rudiments of the world,” the elementary, worldly principles — the ceremonial system and its like. They mark the force of the question: if you have died to these things, why “as though living in the world” — as if still under its dominion — do you “subject yourselves to ordinances”? It is inconsistent for the dead to be bound by the rules of the life they have left. They observe Calvin’s point that we are dead to the law and the law to us; the relationship is dissolved, so its ordinances have no further claim. They note that to return to ceremonial observances is to act as though the death with Christ had not happened, to put on again the grave-clothes one has cast off. The verse thus turns the doctrine of union with Christ’s death into a practical argument: having died to the world’s elements, the believer must not live as their subject — and the absurdity of doing so is exposed in the specimen rules that follow.
21:
On Colossians 2:21 — “(Touch not; taste not; handle not” — the witnesses mark the specimen of ascetic rules.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the prohibitions. Robertson: “handle not, nor taste, nor touch” (me hapse mede geuse mede thiges) — “specimens of Gnostic rules.” Ellicott reverses the rendering: a climax of strictness — “handle not… taste not… touch not” with a finger; all negative. Adam Clarke: forms of expression common among the Jews. Wesley: an unclean thing, forbidden meat, a consecrated vessel.

The verse quotes the ascetics’ rules. “Do not handle, do not taste, do not touch.” Paul cites their prohibitions to expose their hollowness.

So the witnesses see Paul mockingly quote the petty prohibitions of the false teachers. The witnesses note that these are not Paul’s own commands but a specimen of the ascetic rules the deceivers imposed — the very voice of the legalists, with their string of negatives. They mark Ellicott’s point about the climax of strictness in the original order: do not even handle the forbidden thing, do not taste it, do not so much as touch it with a fingertip — a mounting severity over food, drink, and ritual purity. They observe that all the rules are negative, prohibitions and abstentions, a religion of “thou shalt not” concerning external things. They note that such regulations recall the Jewish scruples about clean and unclean and the ascetic taboos of the philosophy, all multiplied and tightened. They observe that Paul cites them precisely to expose their character: a religion of fussy abstentions about perishable matters, having nothing to do with the spiritual kingdom of God. The verse thus holds up the false teaching in its own words — a tangle of petty prohibitions — preparing Paul’s twofold refutation: that these things perish in the using, and that they rest on human commandment, not divine.

So the witnesses see Paul mockingly quote the petty prohibitions of the false teachers. These are not Paul’s own commands but a specimen of the ascetic rules the deceivers imposed — the very voice of the legalists, with their string of negatives. Ellicott notes the climax of strictness in the original order: do not even handle the forbidden thing, do not taste it, do not so much as touch it with a fingertip. All the rules are negative, prohibitions and abstentions, a religion of “thou shalt not” about external things. Paul cites them to expose their character: fussy abstentions about perishable matters, having nothing to do with the spiritual kingdom of God.
22:
On Colossians 2:22 — “Which all are to perish with the using;) after the commandments and doctrines of men” — the witnesses mark the twofold refutation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the argument. Robertson: “are to perish with the using” (phthoran te apochresei) — decay in the consuming. Ellicott: the things “go to corruption in the using.” Adam Clarke: “not matters of eternal moment… made for the body.” The Reformed Calvin: he sets them aside by a twofold argument — they are outward and frail, and from men, not God.

The verse refutes the rules on two grounds. The forbidden things perish in the using, and the rules rest on human commands and teachings. Both their object and their source are worthless.

So the witnesses see Paul demolish the ascetic rules with a double argument. The witnesses note the first ground: these forbidden things “are to perish with the using” — the foods and material objects fussed over are perishable, decaying in the very act of being consumed; they belong to the body and pass away, having no bearing on the eternal, spiritual kingdom of God. They mark that to make religion consist in such trivial, transient matters is to mistake its very nature. They observe the second ground: these rules are “after the commandments and doctrines of men” — merely human inventions, not divine requirements; they lack God’s authority. They note Calvin’s summary: Paul sets aside the enactments because they concern things outward and frail, and because they are from men, not from God — the same charge Christ made against the traditions of the Pharisees. They observe that these two arguments together strip the rules of all weight: their object is perishable, their source merely human. The verse thus completes the exposure of the false asceticism: it binds the conscience over perishing trifles by mere human authority — a religion both petty in its objects and groundless in its origin, which the next verse will allow only a hollow show of wisdom.

So the witnesses see Paul demolish the ascetic rules with a double argument. The first ground: these forbidden things “are to perish with the using” — the foods and objects fussed over are perishable, decaying in the very act of being consumed; they belong to the body and pass away, with no bearing on the eternal kingdom. The second: these rules are “after the commandments and doctrines of men” — merely human inventions, lacking God’s authority, the same charge Christ made against the Pharisees’ traditions. Together these strip the rules of all weight: their object perishable, their source merely human.
23:
On Colossians 2:23 — “Which things have indeed a shew of wisdom in will worship, and humility, and neglecting of the body; not in any honour to the satisfying of the flesh” — the witnesses mark the hollow show of ascetic wisdom.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the appearance. Robertson: “have indeed a show of wisdom” (logon…sophias, the repute of wisdom); “will-worship” (self-imposed). Adam Clarke: “a show of wisdom… a worship which God has not commanded.” Wesley: “not of any real value before God… they satisfy the flesh.” The Reformed Calvin: he concedes the appearance but reckons it worthless.

The verse grants the rules a show of wisdom but denies them value. They have an appearance of wisdom in self-chosen worship, humility, and bodily severity, yet are of no real worth and do not curb the flesh. The show is all there is.

So the witnesses see Paul concede the false asceticism a mere appearance while denying it all substance. The witnesses note the admission: these things “have indeed a shew of wisdom” — a reputation, a plausible appearance of piety and profundity that impresses the undiscerning. They mark its three forms: “will worship” — self-devised, self-imposed religion, worship God has not commanded; “humility” — the affected lowliness already exposed; and “neglecting of the body” — harsh severity to the flesh, asceticism that looks impressively spiritual. They observe the decisive verdict: “not in any honour” — of no real value before God — “to the satisfying of the flesh.” The expositors read this difficult clause to mean that, for all its severity, this self-denial does not truly check the flesh but rather feeds it — gratifying pride, self-will, and the desire to appear distinguished. They note Wesley’s point that such practices indulge the corrupt nature even while afflicting the body. They observe the irony: a system that looks like the height of wisdom and mortification is in truth worthless and even nourishes the very flesh it pretends to subdue. The verse thus closes the great refutation: the false teaching has only a show of wisdom; its will-worship and bodily severity honor no one and conquer nothing — leaving the believer to seek true holiness, as the next chapter shows, in the risen Christ.
